images.caption:
Figure 1:Experimental framework for analyzing self-report–behavior
coherence in LLMs.We investigate(RQ1)whether self-reports
predict behavior under ideal conditions, then relax along three axes:(RQ2)fine-grained TPB→\tocoarse-grained Big Five;(RQ3)within-session→\tobetween-session;(RQ4)parameter grid→\topersona grid. Evaluation spans 4 behavioral tasks and 11 LLMs.

header: 2RQ1. Shared-Context: Does Self-Report Predict Behavior at All?

images.caption: Figure 2:RQ1: Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB) self-report substantially predicts behavior under same-session conditions, with task-specific patterns matching TPB’s theoretical scope.Within-model Pearson correlations between TPB self-report and behavior; grid induction,∼\sim54 observations per cell.(A)Per-task Fisher-zzmeanrrfor Intention (grey) vs. theoretically-primary construct (orange); IAT inversion is theoretically expected (compensatory effort to supress bias).(B)Per-model Fisher-zzaggregated meanrr; per-bar annotations showrrvalues (↑\uparrowTPB predicts task behavior better).(C)Per-cell within-modelrrheatmap; rows sorted by panel B;∗*marksp<.05p<.05.

Figure 3:RQ2: TPB substantially outperforms Big Five in predicting LLM behavior under within-session, parameter-grid conditions.(A1, A2)Per-task Fisher-zzaggregated meanralignedr_{\text{aligned}}(sign-corrected to theoretical direction) for TPB constructs (orange) and Big Five traits (blue).(B)Per-model TPB vs. Big Five comparison; models sorted by TPBralignedr_{\text{aligned}}descending.(C)Big Five per-cell within-modelralignedr_{\text{aligned}}heatmap. TPB orange bars are substantially positive across all volitional tasks and most models; Big Five blue bars and heatmap cells are near zero throughout.

header: 3RQ2. Framework Specificity: Does TPB Granularity Outperform Big Five?

images.caption: Figure 4:RQ3: Context separation collapses TPB-behavior coherence for most models, with Sycophancy showing the sharpest collapse.All panels: TPB constructs, grid perturbation only.(A1, A2)Per-task Fisher-zzmeanralignedr_{\text{aligned}}for TPB Intention (light) and theoretically-primary construct (dark), in same-session (orange) vs. separate-sessions (teal).(B)Per-model TPBralignedr_{\text{aligned}}pooled across task×\timesconstruct cells; same-session (orange) vs. separate-sessions (teal). Models with separate-sessions 95% CI above zero are highlighted in light gray.(C1, C2)Per-(model×\timestask) cross-session correlation: Self-Report (SR) consistency (left) and Behavior consistency (right). Sycophancy behavior shows systematic anti-correlation across sessions, consistent with context-window priming.

header: 4RQ3. Context Separation: Does Coherence Survive Session Separation?
5RQ4. Identity Induction: Does Persona Grounding Rescue Coherence?

images.caption: Figure 5:RQ4: persona induction does not rescue separate-sessions coherence, despite producing more diverse and more stable SR profiles.All panels: TPB constructs, separate-sessions only.(A1, A2)Per-task Fisher-zzmeanralignedr_{\text{aligned}}for TPB Intention (light) and theoretically-primary construct (dark), under grid (teal) vs. persona (purple) induction.(B)Per-model paired bars: separate-sessions grid (teal) vs. personas (purple).(C1, C2)Per-(model×\timestask)Δ\Deltaheatmaps:C1= SR diversity (personas SD−-grid SD;>0{>}0: more varied profiles);C2= SR stability (personasrr−-gridrr;>0{>}0: more consistent across sessions). Mostly-blue C1/C2 shows personas improved SR properties.

header: Appendix DBehavioral Tasks: Selection, Operationalisation, and Construct Mappings

images.caption:
Figure 6:Big Five self-report fingerprints across 11 models
(within-session).Each panel shows one model’s mean Likert score (1–5
scale) across the five Big Five traits under parameter-grid (blue) and
persona (red) inductions. Shaded regions:±1\pm 1SD across conditions.

Figure 7:TPB self-report fingerprints, parameter-grid induction
(within-session).Each panel: one model. X-axis: 4 TPB constructs (Att =
Attitude, SN = Subjective Norm, PBC = Perceived Behavioral Control,
Int = Intention). Lines coloured by behavioral task. Shaded:±1\pm 1SD
across conditions.

Figure 8:TPB self-report fingerprints, persona induction
(within-session).Same structure as Figure7but
under persona prompting. Bands are visibly wider than under grid
induction, consistent with the SR-diversity gain reported in
§5(Fig.5C1).

Figure 9:Behavioral fingerprints under same-session probing.Each panel: one model’s mean behavioral score across the 5 dimensions
(Risk Taking, Sycophancy, Epistemic Honesty, Self-Reflective Honesty,
Stereotyping), mapped to a 1–5 common scale (Table3).
Lines coloured by induction (Grid: blue; Personas: red). Shaded:±1\pm 1SD across conditions. Dotted line at 3.0 marks the neutral/mid point per
task.

Figure 10:Behavioral fingerprints under separate-sessions probing.Same structure as Figure9, but with self-report and
behavior elicited in independent conversations. Compare the Sycophancy
column: several models that suppressed sycophantic deferral under
same-session probing now show high deferral rates, consistent with RQ3’s
finding that same-session Sycophancy coherence depends on the self-report
being visible in the prompt window when the behavioral choice is made.

header: Appendix FConstruct Validity: Internal Reliability of TPB and Big Five Instruments
Appendix IRQ3 & RQ4: Cross-Session Analysis and Persona Induction

images.caption:
Figure 11:Full session×\timesinduction interaction for TPBtheoretically primaryconstruct(CCT→\rightarrowPBC,
Sycophancy→\rightarrowSubjective Norm, Honesty→\rightarrowAttitude,
IAT→\rightarrowIntention). Four bars per task: within-grid (dark
orange), within-personas (light orange), between-grid (dark teal),
between-personas (light teal). Error bars are 95% Fisher-z CIs.
Cross-reference: main-text Figure5(A) shows
between-session cells only, for both TPB and Big5, using the
best-baseline construct per cell rather than the theoretical primary.

Figure 12:RQ4 prerequisites.(A)Discriminability: mean within-model SR-SD across conditions
per framework–construct, parameter grid (dark) vs. persona grounding
(light); pooled across tasks, within-session only. Stars indicate
paired-Wilcoxonp<.05p<.05/<.01<.01/<.001<.001across models.(B)Stability: Fisher-z aggregatedr​(SRwithin,SRbetween)r(\text{SR}_{\text{within}},\text{SR}_{\text{between}})across
matched conditions, with 95% CI. Persona induction produces
significantly higher cross-session SR consistency than parameter
variation for every TPB construct and for the Big5 traits with the
strongest personality content.

Figure 13:Between-model SR–behavior scatter plots: TPB constructs,
parameter-grid induction (n=11n=11models per panel).Each dot is one model; axes are model-mean z-standardised self-report
(x) and model-mean z-standardised behavior (y).
Regression slopeβ^between\hat{\beta}_{\text{between}}(Mundlak pooled OLS,
cluster-robust SEs) is annotated in each panel and matches
Table12.
Notable cells: CCT×\timesIntentionβ^between=+0.24∗\hat{\beta}_{\text{between}}=+0.24^{*}(the only TPB between-model effect reaching significance); Honesty×\timesAttitudeβ^between=+0.14\hat{\beta}_{\text{between}}=+0.14(positive but non-significant withn=11n=11, consistent with the within-model effect dominating); IAT×\timesIntentionβ^between=−0.03\hat{\beta}_{\text{between}}=-0.03(essentially flat between models, in
contrast to the largeβ^within=−0.63∗⁣∗∗\hat{\beta}_{\text{within}}=-0.63^{***}— the
explicit–implicit inversion is awithin-modelphenomenon, not a
between-model trait difference).

Figure 14:Between-model SR–behavior scatter plots: Big Five traits,
parameter-grid induction (n=11n=11models per panel).Layout mirrors Figure13.β^between\hat{\beta}_{\text{between}}values and shading match
Table12: panels shadedgreencorrespond to cells with a positive (theory-consistent)β^between\hat{\beta}_{\text{between}}in Table12; panels shadedredcorrespond to theory-inconsistentnegativeβ^between\hat{\beta}_{\text{between}}.
For the Honesty task, the outcome variable is the calibration scorebrier_c1(lower Brier score = better calibration = more epistemically
honest), not the signed confidence-change metric|Δ​conf||\Delta\text{conf}|; theβ^between\hat{\beta}_{\text{between}}reported here matches the paper’s specification.
The strongest between-model Big Five signal is CCT×\timesNeuroticism
(β^between=+0.63∗∗\hat{\beta}_{\text{between}}=+0.63^{**}): models with higher mean Neuroticism
self-report take less risk on average across conditions; all other Big Five
between-model effects are non-significant.

Figure 15:Between-model SR–behavior scatter plots: TPB constructs,
persona induction (n=11n=11models per panel).Same layout and estimand as Figure13.β^between\hat{\beta}_{\text{between}}values are computed from the Mundlak pooled OLS
decomposition under persona induction and correspond to the persona arm
of Table12. The broad pattern – positive slopes
on CCT and Honesty, near-flat slope on IAT – is preserved relative to the
grid induction, indicating that between-model identity differences in
SR and behavior are stable across induction method.

Figure 16:Between-model SR–behavior scatter plots: Big Five traits,
persona induction (n=11n=11models per panel).Same layout and shading convention as Figure14.
Honesty outcome isbrier_c1as in the grid variant.
Persona prompting does not rescue Big Five’s between-model predictive
signal: the pattern of significant and nullβ^between\hat{\beta}_{\text{between}}cells remains similar to the grid-induction case, and no cell reaches
significance that was null under grid induction.

Figure 17:Within-model SR–behavior scatter plots: TPB constructs,
parameter-grid induction (n≈594n\approx 594demeaned observations per panel).Both axes demeaned by per-model mean;rris Fisher-zztransformed.
Circle = Policy A, triangle = Policy B.Blue-shaded panelsmark the theoretically-primary TPB construct per
task (Table1): IAT×\timesIntention (r=−0.59r=-0.59,[−0.64,−0.53][-0.64,-0.53]); Honesty×\timesAttitude (r=+0.67∗⁣∗∗r=+0.67^{***},[+0.63,+0.72][+0.63,+0.72]); Sycophancy×\timesSubjective Norm (r=+0.19∗⁣∗∗r=+0.19^{***},[+0.10,+0.27][+0.10,+0.27]); CCT×\timesPBC (r=+0.14∗∗r=+0.14^{**},[+0.05,+0.22][+0.05,+0.22]).
Three structural patterns are visible across all panels.
(i)CCT is the only task with genuine within-policy covariation:
the cloud shows a smooth positive trend even within each policy-symbol
cluster, consistent with temperature and seed varying CCT decision-making
continuously; all other CCT constructs show the same gradient (Intentionr=+0.22∗⁣∗∗r=+0.22^{***}, Attituder=+0.02r=+0.02, PBCr=+0.14∗∗r=+0.14^{**}).
(ii)Bimodal clustering in IAT, Honesty, and Sycophancy: dots
form two well-separated clouds (one per policy symbol) rather than a
continuous distribution; the overallrrtherefore reflects across-policy step, not a smooth within-policy SR–behavior gradient
(cf. AppendixG.4).
(iii)IAT within-model inversionis consistent across all four
TPB constructs (Intention−0.59-0.59, Attitude−0.74-0.74, SN−0.81-0.81, PBC+0.08+0.08), the clearest exception being PBC where the inversion is absent,
reproducing the compensatory-effort pattern discussed in §2.

Figure 18:Within-model SR–behavior scatter plots: Big Five traits,
parameter-grid induction (n≈297n\approx 297–378378demeaned observations per
panel).Both axes demeaned by per-model mean.
The annotated statistic israligned=signtheory×r​(trait,raw_beh)r_{\text{aligned}}=\text{sign}_{\text{theory}}\times r(\text{trait},\text{raw\_beh}), matching the paper’s Big Five
sign convention exactly.
All panels display near-zeroralignedr_{\text{aligned}}, confirming the main-text
RQ2 claim that Big Five fails to predict behavior at the condition level
within models.
The CCT×\timesNeuroticism cell (raligned=+0.03r_{\text{aligned}}=+0.03) illustrates
this null starkly: the theoretical direction is negative (higher Neuroticism→\toless risk taking), yet the observedralignedr_{\text{aligned}}is positive
and near zero, with 95% CI crossing zero.

Figure 19:Within-model SR–behavior scatter plots: TPB constructs,
persona induction (n≈660n\approx 660demeaned observations per panel).Same layout, demeaning, and blue-shading convention as
Figure17; 30 PersonaHub character
descriptions replace parameter-grid conditions.
Primary-cell Fisher-zzrrvalues under persona induction:
IAT×\timesIntention (r=−0.64r=-0.64,[−0.68,−0.59][-0.68,-0.59]);
Honesty×\timesAttitude (r=+0.53∗⁣∗∗r=+0.53^{***},[+0.47,+0.58][+0.47,+0.58]);
Sycophancy×\timesSubjective Norm (r=+0.02r=+0.02,[−0.06,+0.09][-0.06,+0.09], ns);
CCT×\timesPBC (r=+0.21∗⁣∗∗r=+0.21^{***},[+0.13,+0.28][+0.13,+0.28]).
Two changes from the grid induction stand out.
First,Sycophancy×\timesSubjective Norm drops fromr=+0.19∗⁣∗∗r=+0.19^{***}tor=+0.02r=+0.02(ns): persona prompting does not
preserve the within-model coupling on this primary cell, consistent with
the RQ4 finding that persona induction fails to rescue SR–behavior
coherence for Sycophancy (Table14).
Second,Honesty×\timesAttitude attenuates from+0.67+0.67to+0.53+0.53, mirroring the partial attenuation reported in
Table13.
CCT×\timesPBC is stable (+0.14+0.14to+0.21+0.21) and IAT×\timesIntention is stable (−0.59-0.59to−0.64-0.64), consistent with those tasks’
preserved cross-session coherence.
The bimodal within-model clustering on IAT, Honesty, and Sycophancy
panels and CCT’s continuous within-policy gradient both replicate the
grid-induction structure.

Figure 20:Within-model SR–behavior scatter plots: Big Five traits,
persona induction (n≈297n\approx 297–378378demeaned observations per panel).Same layout as Figure18;raligned=signtheory×r​(trait,raw_beh)r_{\text{aligned}}=\text{sign}_{\text{theory}}\times r(\text{trait},\text{raw\_beh})computed within the persona-induction arm.
As under grid induction, allralignedr_{\text{aligned}}values are near zero
and no cell shows a significant within-model Big Five–behavior coupling.
Persona prompting does not restore predictive power at the condition level:
the surface-level SR shifts induced by character descriptions (documented
in Fig.5C1) do not translate into a detectable
within-model SR–behavior gradient for any Big Five trait.

Figure 21:TPB.Three rows per task: between-session behavior
(grey, top), within-session behavior split by Policy A vs. B (middle), SR
Attitude split by Policy A vs. B (bottom). Right-side gutter reports|d||d|Cohen’sddand raw policy means in native units (Likert 1–7 for SR;
task-native units for Behavior). Headline ratioRSR/Beh\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{SR/Beh}}classifies tasks into the priming regime
(R<<1, Sycophancy and CCT) vs. the dispositional/decoupling regime (R>>1,
Honesty and IAT). Stars mark Wilcoxon paired tests of|d|Beh|d|_{\mathrm{Beh}}vs.|d|SR|d|_{\mathrm{SR}}.

Figure 22:Big Five.Three rows per task: between-session behavior (grey, top); within-session behavior (single dark distribution, since Big Five SR is policy-agnostic); SR for the two theoretically-motivated Big Five traits per task (bottom). Traitzz-scores are sign-flipped so that high-trait-along-its-theory-direction matches Policy A direction on the behavior rows above, allowing within-column visual comparison across frameworks.

header: Appendix JPersona Induction: Selection Procedure and Stimulus Set
